coredata.dc:title: Novel Co2+ passivated carbon nanodots with up-conversion effects combined with NH2-MIL-125 for improving photocatalytic NO purification and hydrogen evolution
coredata.dc:creator: He, Youzhou
Luo, Shuang
Tan, Yuwei
Chen, Mingzuo
Zhou, Haibo
Yu, Jianning
Pu, Linjiang
Huang, Yumin
Fu, Min
Liu, Xingyan
coredata.dc:description: Novel ionic state Co passivated carbon nanodots (Co-CDs) combined with NH2-MIL-125 as efficient photocatalysts Co-CDs/NH2-MIL-125 were fabricated in a convenient process for the first time. These materials exhibited excellent photocatalytic activity in both NO purification and hydrogen production under visible light irradiation. The NO purification rate of Co-CDs/NH2-MIL-125 increased to 46.6%, which was 20.2% and 13.4% higher than that of NH2-MIL-125 and CDs/NH2-MIL-125, respectively. Moreover, the hydrogen production efficiency of Co-CDs/NH2-MIL-125 reached 2261 μmol g−1, which was 6.86 times and 3.54 times of the values for NH2-MIL-125 and CDs/NH2-MIL-125, respectively. The corresponding properties characterized by Mott-Schottky, UCPL, and in situ DRIFTS, indicated that the introduction of Co2+ could not only passivate the CDs to further improve physicochemical properties, but also enhance the photocatalytic activity via the photosensitization and up-conversion effect. This research provides a promising strategy that adding active metal ions passivated CDs in the catalyst platform to improve photocatalytic performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NH2-MIL-125
Co passivated carbon nanodots
Up-conversion effect
NO purification
Hydrogen production

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: PL, EIS, transient photocurrent response curves and LSV analysis
The ESR spectra and in situ DRIFTS spectra

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FT-IR spectra of as-prepared catalysts; (b) FT-IR spectra of CDs and Co-CDs.
(a) The HRTEM images of Co-CDs; (b) particle size distribution of Co-CDs; (c-d) the HRTEM images of CDs.

(a) PL spectra of as-prepared catalysts; (b) the transient photocurrents of as-prepared catalysts; (c) EIS spectra of as-prepared catalysts; (d) the UCPL spectra of CDs under 600, 700 and 800 nm; (e) the UCPL spectra of Co-CDs under 600, 700 and 800 nm.
(a) Photocatalytic NO purification and (b) photocatalytic hydrogen production performance under visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm) for 4 h.
ESR spectra of as-prepared catalysts for (a) •OH /DMPO and (b) •O2 -/DMPO; (c-f) in situ DRIFTS spectra of NO adsorption and visible light reaction processes over NH2-MIL-125 and Co-CDs/NM.
Possible photocatalytic mechanism of Co-CDs/NM under visible light.

head.ce:title: Novel Co2+ passivated carbon nanodots with up-conversion effects combined with NH2-MIL-125 for improving photocatalytic NO purification and hydrogen evolution

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work and should be considered co-ﬁrst authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel ionic state Co passivated carbon nanodots (Co-CDs) combined with NH2-MIL-125 as efficient photocatalysts Co-CDs/NH2-MIL-125 were fabricated in a convenient process for the first time. These materials exhibited excellent photocatalytic activity in both NO purification and hydrogen production under visible light irradiation. The NO purification rate of Co-CDs/NH2-MIL-125 increased to 46.6%, which was 20.2% and 13.4% higher than that of NH2-MIL-125 and CDs/NH2-MIL-125, respectively. Moreover, the hydrogen production efficiency of Co-CDs/NH2-MIL-125 reached 2261 μmol g−1, which was 6.86 times and 3.54 times of the values for NH2-MIL-125 and CDs/NH2-MIL-125, respectively. The corresponding properties characterized by Mott-Schottky, UCPL, and in situ DRIFTS, indicated that the introduction of Co2+ could not only passivate the CDs to further improve physicochemical properties, but also enhance the photocatalytic activity via the photosensitization and up-conversion effect. This research provides a promising strategy that adding active metal ions passivated CDs in the catalyst platform to improve photocatalytic performance.

The efficient photocatalytic material Co-CDs/NH2-MIL-125 was brought up for the first time, which could exhibit excellent photocatalytic activity in both NO purification and hydrogen production under visible light irradiation.
ga1

ce:section.ce:para: Environment pollution and global energy crisis are becoming very thorny problems in recent years and puzzling scientist around the world [1,2]. Innovative technologies, such as thermal catalysis [3], photocatalysis [4] and electrocatalysis [5,6], have been adopted to solve these problems. Among the feasible techniques, photocatalysis could be a better potential option with many merits such as availability of resources, relatively safe operation and environment friendliness [7]. Recently, some researches on photocatalytic air purification (particularly the removal of NO, one of the main types of air pollution) [8,9] and photocatalytic hydrogen production (hydrogen, a perfect alternative energy carrier of clean energy instead of fossil fuels) [10,11] have attracted a great interest. The performance of photocatalytic NO purification and hydrogen production has been improved simultaneously, providing a special method to solve environmental problems and energy crises to a certain extent. However, the photocatalytic activity of most existing photocatalytic materials is still relatively poor due to the poor light absorption ability, fast electron-hole recombination, and narrow light response range [12,13]. Therefore, it is very urgent to develop an efficient photocatalytic material that can successfully address the environmental pollution and energy shortage issues to achieve "source control" and "end-of-pipe treatment" strategies simultaneously [14].

Metal organic frameworks (MOFs) constitute of the metal ions/clusters and organic bridging linkers, and are considered as efficient photocatalysts to solve the environmental contamination and energy shortage [15–19]. Among a series of MOFs, NH2-MIL-125 with an appropriate bandgap (2.6 eV) has a large specific surface area, which can provide abundant active sites on their surface and facilitate interfacial reactions with reactants. Therefore, such MOFs has been widely used in the field of catalysis [20,21]. Despite of the potential advantages of NH2-MIL-125, the large-scale application in photocatalysis is still limited due to a difficult separation of charge carriers and weak light absorption. It has been demonstrated that some carbon-based materials could be used as efficient electron reservoirs to capture photoinduced electrons from NH2-MIL-125 to extend the lifetime of photogenerated carriers, thereby improving the photocatalytic activity of target reactions [22,23].

Carbon nanodots (CDs), one type of carbon-based materials, have endowed several favorable attributes, especially including the unique up-conversion photoluminescence (UCPL) property and excellent photogenerated carriers transfer capability[24–27]. However, the low quantum yields (QYs) and insufficient number of surface functional groups of the original CDs are the two main obstacles limiting their applications [28]. Chemical coupling with heteroatoms (N, S, B, etc.) is normally used to tune the electronic and optical characteristics of CDs, especially doping active metal ions into CDs to establish a new route to optimize CDs-based catalysts [29]. In comparison with most nonmetallic heteroatoms, metal ions (especially transition metal ions) often have unfilled orbitals and unique electron configurations, which facilitates the rapid migration of electrons. Once metal ions are coupled with CDs to integrate metal-CDs (M-CDs), charge transition modes between the metal ions and CDs will be altered, and such alternation could be beneficial to optimize the physicochemical properties of CDs. So far, a photocatalyst consisting of M-CDs with MOFs that can exhibit excellent photocatalytic activity in both NO purification and hydrogen production has not been reported.

Herein, Co-CDs/NH2-MIL-125 (abbreviation Co-CDs/NM), an efficient photocatalyst was synthesized via a simple procedure combining the novel Co2+ passivated CDs (Co-CDs) with NH2-MIL-125, which shows excellent photocatalytic activity in both NO purification and hydrogen production. Compared with the original NH2-MIL-125 and CDs/NH2-MIL-125 (abbreviation CDs/NM), it was found that the photocatalytic performance of Co-CDs/NM was significantly enhanced after the introduction of Co ions into CDs. In addition, the synergistic effects between the Co-CDs and NH2-MIL-125 have been studied deeply via a series of characterization experiments to confirm the importance of ionic Co. In this study, we present a strategy to fabricate M-CDs/MOFs with improved photocatalytic performance towards both NO purification and hydrogen production.

Glucose (Aladdin, 98%), cobalt (II) gluconate hydrate (Alfa, 98%), 2-aminoterephthalic acid (Adamas, 98%), N, N-dimethylformamide (Adamas, 99%), titanium isopropoxide (Aladdin, 99%). All chemicals were used without further puriﬁcation.

NH2-MIL-125 was synthesized according to the previous report [30]. 2.86 g (15.8 mmol) of 2-aminoterephthalic acid was dissolved in a mixture of 40 mL dry N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) and 10 mL dry methanol at room temperature. Then 2.86 mL (9.7 mmol) of titanium isopropoxide was added and the mixture solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (100 mL) and heated at 110 °C for 3 days. The products were collected by filtration and kept stirring in 50 mL DMF overnight to remove unreacted reagents. The same procedure was then repeated twice using anhydrous methanol instead of DMF to exchange DMF in the wells. To finally achieve sample activation, the as-synthesized solids were dried overnight at 100 °C in a vacuum desiccator.

Glucose was calcined at 240 °C in a nitrogen atmosphere for 2 h. The obtained black solid was stirred in the aqueous solution for 1 h, the clear liquid was collected by suction filtration, and the clear liquid was freeze-dried to obtain CDs.

Cobalt (II) gluconate hydrate was calcined at 240 °C in a nitrogen atmosphere for 2 h. The obtained taupe solid was stirred in the aqueous solution for 1 h, the clear liquid was collected by suction filtration, and the clear liquid was freeze-dried to obtain Co-CDs.

The CDs (1.5 mg) were dissolved in 30 mL deionized water, and NH2-MIL-125 (300 mg) was mixed in the CDs aqueous solutions to obtain a yellow solution, then the solution was stirred at room temperature for 6 h, centrifuged and washed with water, and dried the yellow solid in a vacuum for one day to obtain CDs/NM.

The Co-CDs (1.5 mg) were dissolved in 30 mL deionized water, and NH2-MIL-125 (300 mg) was mixed in the Co-CDs aqueous solutions to obtain a yellow mixed solution, then this solution was stirred at room temperature for 6 h, centrifuged and washed with water, and dried the yellow solid in a vacuum for one day to obtain Co-CDs/NM ( Fig. 1).

Various characterization techniques have been used to evaluate physical and chemical properties of the photocatalysts. Microstructure and morphology of Co-CDs/NM were characterized by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns (XPD-6100, Shimadzu, Japan), energy dispersive X-Ray spectroscopy (EDX) (FEI Quanta 650FEG, USA), scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs (Zeiss Merlin, Germany), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra (IRPrestige-21, Shimadzu, Japan), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) experiment (FEI Tecnai G2 20, USA), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, USA), and Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) measurements (ASAP 2020, USA). Photocatalytic properties and mechanisms were investigated by photoluminescence spectrum (PL spectrum) (F-7000, HITACHI, Japan), UV–vis diffuse reﬂectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS) (UV–vis DRS, Shimadzu, Japan), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) (CHI600E), transient photocurrent response curve (PEC 1000, CHI600E), Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) (CHI600E), Mott-Schottky (CHI600E); electron spin-resonance spectroscopy (ESR) (FA200, HITACHI, Japan), up-conversion photoluminescence spectra (UCPL) (F-4600, HITACHI, Japan) and diffused reflectance infrared fourier transform spectroscopy (DRIFTS) (Bruker, Germany).

The photocatalytic activity of the prepared samples was tested by eliminating NO at the ppb level in a continuous flow reactor. The reactor contains a rectangular glass box with a capacity of 4.5 L and two ordinaries LED lamps (12 W) placed vertically above the reactor. Typically, the catalysts (100 mg) were weighed separately and placed in two sample dishes with a diameter of 12 cm. The test catalysts were dispersed by adding ethanol (about 10 mL) to the above sample dishes and then sonicating for 10 min. The catalysts were sonicated and dried until all the solvent was evaporated, and then cooled to room temperature. The processed samples were placed in the middle of the reactor. The initial concentration of NO, which was obtained from a compressed gas cylinder with a concentration of 100 ppm (N2 balance), was diluted to 520 ppb. The flow rates of NO and air were controlled at 15 mL/min and 2.4 L/min, respectively. The lamp was turned on for illumination after the glass container reached adsorption-desorption equilibrium. The NO concentration was always detected using a NOx analyzer (Thermo Scientific, 42i-TL). The analyzer also monitored the concentration of NO2, NOx (NOx stands for NO2 + NO).

The photocatalytic activity of the as-synthesized samples was assessed by hydrogen formation under visible light through water splitting. 30 mg catalysts were uniformly dispersed in 17 mL acetonitrile and 0.2 mL deionized water, and 2 mL triethanolamine was used as an electron sacrificial agent in the hydrogen production system. Subsequently, 1 mL acetonitrile solution containing a certain amount of H2PtCl6 was added as a precursor of the cocatalyst Pt (1.25 wt%) in situ during the photoreductions reaction. Then, the reaction solution was bubbled with nitrogen gas to completely remove the air for at least 0.5 h. Finally, the sealed reactor was irradiated under visible light by using a 300 W Xe lamp equipped with a 380 nm cut-off filter under vigorous stirring, and the reaction temperature was always maintained at 10 °C. During the visible light irradiation, the evolved amount of hydrogen was collected at given time intervals and analyzed using a gas chromatograph (GC 7920, Beijing China Education Au-light Co., Ltd.) equipped with a thermally conductive detector and a high purity N2 carrier gas.

We used the TENSOR II FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker) equipped with an in situ diffuse-reflectance cell (Harrick) and a high-temperature reaction chamber (HVC) to conduct in situ DRIFTS measurements. Before measurements, the as-prepared samples were placed in a vacuum tube and pretreated for 1 h at 110 ℃. The background spectra were recorded before injecting NO into the reaction chamber. And the NO absorption bands appeared once NO came in contact with the photocatalyst at 25 ℃ under dark conditions. Once the adsorption equilibrium was achieved, a visible-light source was applied to initiate the photocatalytic reaction. And the baseline was the same as the curve of adsorption equilibrium in the NO adsorption process. As for the temporal evolution of normalized absorbance about adsorbed species on photocatalysts surface, the integration of characteristic absorption bands of adsorbed species leads to following their individual evolutions as a function of time. For all species, the normalized absorbance was calculated by considering their individual maximum absorbance as 1.

The crystal structure of the as-synthesized NH2-MIL-125 (Fig. S1) was consistent with the reported simulated XRD data of NH2-MIL-125, and the most dominant characteristic peaks appeared at 6.8°, 9.8° and 11.7°, which correspond to (101), (002) and (211) planes, respectively [30]. After introducing CDs, it could be found that the XRD peak shape of CDs/NM remained unaltered while the peak intensity was reduced, which could be due to the tiny particle size of CDs and amorphous phase of the CDs. [31]. Interestingly, no obvious characteristic peaks of Co clusters or oxides were observed in the XRD pattern of Co-CDs/NM, indicating that the Co element might exist in the form of ionic state in Co-CDs [29]. It has been reported that the metal ions coordinate with carboxylic acid can be retained in metal-CDs after calcining the corresponding metal gluconate [32]. Therefore, metal gluconate could be used as an ideal precursor to conveniently synthesize metal ion passivated CDs with a special characteristic.

Fig. 2a has shown that the tensile vibration of -OH at 3391 cm−1 and CO at 1622 cm−1, which were similar to the peaks in NH2-MIL-125 [31,33,34]. However, after the original NH2-MIL-125 combined with CDs and Co-CDs, we observe that the more intensive peaks of -OH, which might be due to the abundant hydroxyl or carboxyl groups existing on the surface of CDs and Co-CDs. Subsequently, the peaks at 3370, 2933 and 1049 cm−1 were corresponded to the stretching vibrations of O-H, C-H and C-O, respectively (Fig. 2b) [32]. The peak at 1609 cm−1 corresponded to the vibration of CO [32], indicating plenty of carboxylate groups on Co-CDs surface, and this revealed that the carboxylate groups in original cobalt (II) gluconate hydrate were retained as many as possible.

As shown in the enlarged SEM image (Fig. S2a), the original NH2-MIL-125 presented a regular particle shape. After combining with CDs or Co-CDs, the resulting composite materials had similar morphologies as original NH2-MIL-125, while the surface of CDs/NM or Co-CDs/NM became significantly rougher (Fig. S2b–c), which is consistent with the TEM observations (Fig. S2d-f). HRTEM image of the Co-CDs was shown in Fig. 3a, from which many tiny Co-CDs of 1–3 nm in size (average size was about 1.8 nm) could be observed (Fig. 3b), and the distance between adjacent lattice spacing of Co-CDs was 0.21 nm, referring to the (100) fact of graphitic structure [29,35,36]. However, the particle size distribution of CDs was chaotic (Fig. 3c–d), even aggregated carbon spheres with a diameter greater than 10 nm have been found. At the same time, we observe the apparent lattice fringes with the distance of 0.21 nm via the HRTEM images of CDs/NM and Co-CDs/NM (Fig. S4), which further confirmed the CDs or Co-CDs were loaded on the outer surface of the original MOFs. Furthermore, the SEM-EDX elemental mapping spectra (Fig. S3a–b) showed that the Co, C, N, Ti and O elements were singly distributed in the Co-CDs/NM sample.

The high-resolution C1s spectrum of NH2-MIL-125 showed three types of peaks, which corresponded to C-C (284.65 eV), C-N (286.17 eV) and -NH2 (288.64 eV), respectively [37] (Fig. S5a). We observe that the C-N bonds in the C1s spectra of CDs/NM and Co-CDs/NM were shifted to the high binding energy region. In the N1s spectra (Fig. S5b), the peaks appeared at 399.29 eV, 400.41 eV and 402.13 eV were corresponding to -NH2, C-N and -N-H+- groups respectively [38]. It was also found that the C-N bonds in the N1s spectra of CDs/NM and Co-CDs/NM were shifted to high binding energy region, indicating the electron cloud density of C and N atoms reduced in CDs/NM and Co-CDs/NM. We thus speculated that the CDs and Co-CDs could interact with –NH2 of NH2-MIL-125 through chemical bond forces like coordination bond [21].

In the Ti 2p orbitals of NH2-MIL-125 (Fig. S5c), 458.84 eV and 464.57 eV with satellite peaks that were associated with Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 electronic configurations, respectively [39]. Comparatively, a corresponding red-shift was observed in the CDs/NM and Co-CDs/NM profiles, which may be caused by the changed chemical micro-environment of Ti (IV) [40]. Fig. S5d showed the O1s spectra, with the peaks appeared at 530.06 eV (Ti-O) and 531.72 eV (-OH) [41].

The surface composition of CDs and Co-CDs was also studied by XPS (Fig. S5f). In the Co 2p orbitals of Co-CDs (Fig. S5e), two peaks assigned to Co 2p1/2 and Co 2p3/2 were located at 797.52 eV and 781.57 eV respectively, indicating the valence state of Co element was considered as CoII [42,43].

Fig. S6 and Table S1 has shown that the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and the pore size distribution of NH2-MIL-125, CDs/NM and Co-CDs/NM, respectively. We found that the BET area and pore size of MOFs hardly changed after combining with CDs and Co-CDs, indicating that most CDs and Co-CDs were loaded on the outer surface area of the MOFs instead of the hole.

The UV–vis spectra of the as-prepared catalysts have been shown in Fig. S7a. After combining with CDs or Co-CDs, the photocatalysts showed an absorption curve similar to that of the original NH2-MIL-125, but the absorption intensities in the visible region have increased. The increasing intensity may be attributed to π→π*transition of CC bond within carbon core and n→π*transition of CO bond from surface groups, respectively [44,45]. The corresponding tauc plots diagram of these materials have been shown in Fig. S7b, which revealed the relevant values of the optical bandgaps. The results showed that the values of the band gaps were about 2.60, 2.56, and 2.52 eV respectively, which indicated that the combination of CDs or Co-CDs with NH2-MIL-125 would affect the band structure of the MOFs. Furthermore, the UV–vis spectra of CDs and Co-CDs were also shown in Fig. S7c, in which Co-CDs had a significantly wider range of light absorption than CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL, EIS, transient photocurrent response curves and LSV analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The charge separation characteristics of these composite materials were studied by PL spectroscopy ( Fig. 4a). Compared with the original NH2-MIL-125, the PL intensity of CDs/NM was reduced, which indicated an effective electron transfer from NH2-MIL-125 to CDs. After the introduction of ionic Co into CDs, the PL intensity of Co-CDs/NM decreased more significantly, which suggested that CDs passivated with ionic Co could indeed adjust the optical performance of MOFs. In addition, the excitation-related PL behaviors of CDs and Co-CDs were also observed in Fig. S8, which may be attributed to the difference in the surface chemical state of CDs and Co-CDs. Compared with Co-CDs, the PL peak width of CDs was wider, and the excitation-related PL profile was more obvious, which may be attributed to the cluttered composition of CDs. Glucose may undergo unavoidable aggregation, polymerization and aldol condensation reaction during the carbonization process, while cobalt gluconate may be slightly carbonized due to passivated CDs through the charge repulsion of Co2+ ion [32].

As shown in Fig. 4b, it could be seen that the introduction of CDs could improve the transient photocurrent of MOFs, which meant that the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers in MOFs was improved. Moreover, the introduction of Co2+ ions into CDs to obtain Co-CDs/NM also led to further enhancement of transient photocurrent of NH2-MIL-125. The charge-transfer resistance (Rct) of NH2-MIL-125 decreased (Fig. 4c) after the introduction of CDs and Co-CDs, which showed that CDs and Co-CDs could increase the charge transfer rate, and the introduction of Co2+ ions into CDs also led to a further reduced Rct. Obviously, CDs/NM and Co-CDs/NM exhibited increases in transient photocurrent and charge transfer rate, which may be due to the dynamic adjustment of the interface performance of NH2-MIL-125 with the introduction of CD and Co-CD, thereby improving the photocatalytic activities. On the other hand, the transient photocurrent and EIS trends of the samples were in good agreement with their photocatalytic performance, which indicated that the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers was an important factor for the photocatalytic performance.

As displayed in Fig. 4d–e, to illustrate the role of CDs and Co-CDs in driving the visible light response of MOFs, we further obtained UCPL data. The UCPL data showed CDs and Co-CDs emitted shorter wavelengths of light when the CDs and Co-CDs were excited under the excitation wavelengths of 600, 700, 800 nm, in which the emitted light concentrated in the UV–vis region through the as-prepared CDs and Co-CDs could be absorbed and utilized again by NH2-MIL-125.

The photocatalytic performance of the prepared photocatalysts was evaluated by the NO purification rate ( Fig. 5a). The adsorption-desorption equilibrium was reached before turning on the lamp. After illuminating about 30 min, the NO puriﬁcation rates of NH2-MIL-125, CDs/NM and Co-CDs/NM reached 26.4%, 33.2% and 46.6% respectively, in line with the above experimental characterization results. More importantly, the NO2 produced by the partial oxidation of NO induced by O2 molecules was further probed (Fig. S9b). We observe that there was tiny amount of NO2 produced when Co-CDs/NM was used to realize NO removal, thus reducing the secondary pollution. The photocatalytic activity of the Co-CDs/NM catalyst reduced 10.5% after four cycles, which could be due to the occupation on active sites by generated NO3-. The photocatalytic activity recovered a lot after refreshing the Co-CDs/NM by washing with ethanol (fifth run in Fig. S9a). Therefore, it was speculated that the photocatalytic activity of Co-CDs/NM could be regenerated by ethanol.

Photocatalytic hydrogen production of the catalysts was assessed utilizing H2PtCl6 as a cocatalyst and triethanolamine as a sacrificial agent (Fig. 5b). A small amount of hydrogen production was discovered over NH2-MIL-125 and CDs/NM. However, Co-CDs/NM showed much higher hydrogen production performance than NH2-MIL-125 and CDs/NM. Specifically, the hydrogen production efficiency of NH2-MIL-125 and CDs/NM reached 329 and 639 μmol g−1 respectively, while the efficiency for Co-CDs/NM reached 2261 μmol g−1, which was about 6.86 times than original NH2-MIL-125.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The ESR spectra and in situ DRIFTS spectra
ce:section.ce:para: ESR technology (DMPO as a capture agent) was used to discover the active species generated under visible light irradiation ( Fig. 6a–b). The •O2 -/DMPO and •OH/DMPO spin adducts were not detected in the darkness. After turning on the light, the characteristic peaks of typical •O2 -/DMPO and •OH/DMPO adducts were observed in the ESR signal, indicating that •O2 - and •OH radicals were generated during the photocatalytic reaction. Furthermore, the signal intensities of •O2 - and •OH radicals generated through Co-CDs/NM were the strongest, indicating that the Co2+ ionic modified Co-CDs enable the Co-CDs/NM catalyst to generate more free radicals, which further resulted in the enhanced photocatalytic performance.

As shown in Fig. S10, two different radical scavengers were used to test the main active free radical. The addition of p-ben-zoquinone (PBQ) had a strong inhibitory effect on the photocatalytic activity (the NO purification rates of NH2-MIL-125, CDs/NM and Co-CDs/NM were 10.4%, 9.4% and 16%, respectively), while the addition of tertbutyl alcohol (TBA) weakly inhibited the photocatalytic activity (the NO purification rates of NH2-MIL-125, CDs/NM and Co-CDs/NM were 22%, 31.3% and 43.2%, respectively). The results showed that •O2 - was the main active free radical under light irradiation.

In order to further investigate the mechanism of NO removal reaction mechanism, in situ DRIFTS were conducted to dynamically explore the reaction intermediates and final products over NH2-MIL-125 and Co-CDs/NM (Fig. 6c–f). As shown in Fig. 6c, the bands of 1338 cm−1 (N2O3) [46], 1318 cm−1 (cis-N2O2 2-) [46], 1282–1127 cm−1 (NO+) [46], 1102 cm−1 (NO-) [47], 1063 cm−1 (NO3 -) [48], and 1016 cm−1 (NO3 -) [48] were observed. While the Co-CDs/NM exhibited a little different NO adsorption pattern: the bands of 1338 cm−1 (N2O3), 1321–1318 cm−1 (cis-N2O2 2-) [46], 1300–1126 cm−1 (NO+) [46], 1102 cm−1 (NO-) and 1084–1010 cm−1 (NO3 -) [46,48] were obtained (Fig. 6e).

As shown in Fig. 6d, with the increase of light irradiation time, the broadband (cis-N2O2 2-) at 1318 cm−1 disappeared rapidly during the photocatalytic NO removal by NH2-MIL-125. Meanwhile, the band of 1063 cm−1 was blue-shifted to 1054 cm−1 (NO3 -), and the band of 1016 cm−1 was red-shifted to 1021 cm−1 (NO3 -). In Fig. 6f, the bands of 1300–1126 cm−1 (NO+), 1046 cm−1 (NO3 -) and 1014 cm−1 (NO3 -) were strengthened a little, and the band at 1321 cm−1 became weaker while the broadband (NO-) at 1102 cm−1 disappeared. Moreover, we also could discover there was an additional band corresponding to 1275 cm−1 (NO2 -) [46], which could be attributed to the photocatalytic oxidation participated by ROS (•O2 -, •OH): NO/NO+/N2O3 + ROS → NO2 -/NO3 - and 2NO- → cis-N2O2 2- + ROS → NO2 -.

Combining the above experimental results and relevant works from literature, the photocatalytic mechanism of Co-CDs/NM was shown in Fig. 7. The photocatalytic unit (ie, metal-oxo cluster) of NH2-MIL-125 was excited by short-wavelength visible light and the photogenerated electron-hole pairs generated (formula (1)). The -NH2 group of NH2-MIL-125 could interact with the -OH/-COOH or -COOCo on the surface of the Co-CDs through chemical bond forces such as hydrogen bond or coordination bond. Therefore, Co-CDs could not only effectively separate the photogenerated electron-hole pair, but also function as localized electron acceptors to collect the electrons to further enhance the photocatalytic activity. At the same time, Co-CDs could also enhance the light absorption range of MOFs through the up-conversion effect, in which the Co-CDs absorbed long-wavelength light and emitted short-wavelength light so that Co-CDs could serve as photosensitizers to absorb the long-wavelength visible light and further transfer relevant energy to NH2-MIL-125.

In the photocatalytic NO removal system, holes (h+) could oxidize the hydroxide (OH-) adsorbed on the surface of CDs/NM to hydroxyl radical (•OH) (formula (2)) [49]. Hydroxyl radicals could oxidize NO molecules into oxidation products NO+, which were further oxidized to nitrate ions (NO3 -), because hydroxyl radicals had a strong oxidizing ability (formulas (3,4)). At the same time, superoxide radicals (•O2 -) could be formed in the photocatalytic process, which could oxidize NO+ to nitrate ions (NO3 -) (formulas (5,6)). Photogenerated electrons (e-) could be captured by H+ in the photocatalytic H2 production system to produce hydrogen (formulas (7,8)). Among both NO purification and hydrogen production, the Co2+ could not only avoid the aggregation of glucose during the process of carbonization but also passivate the surfaces of Co-CDs to further stabilize CDs and accelerate the transfer of photo-generated electrons, thereby improving the photocatalytic performance.
These processes be described by the following equations:

Co-CDs (up-conversion): λ
NIR-visible →λ
UV–visible .
(1) NH2-MIL-125→e- + h+
(2) h+ + OH-→•OH
(3) NO + •OH→NO+ + OH-
(4) NO+ + 2 •OH + 2 OH-→NO3 - + 2H2O
(5) e- + O2→•O2 -
(6) •O2 - + NO+ + e-→NO3 -
(7) H2O→OH- + H+
(8) 2e- + 2H+→H2
Schematic model.

Efficient photocatalytic materials (Co-CDs/NM), novel Co2+ ionic passivated CDs (Co-CDs) combined with NH2-MIL-125, were fabricated through an uncomplicated and convenient process, and such materials exhibited excellent photocatalytic activity in both NO purification and hydrogen production under visible-light irradiation. The Co2+ ionic passivated Co-CDs enhanced the photocatalytic performance of original NH2-MIL-125 significantly (Co-CDs/NM, the H2 evolution efficiency (2261 μmol g−1) and NO purification rate (46.6%); NH2-MIL-125 alone, the H2 evolution efficiency (329 μmol g−1) and NO purification rate (26.4%)). The effective separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers and the up-conversion effect originating from Co-CDs could enhance the photocatalytic activity of Co-CDs/NM. This novel strategy provided an alternative avenue to finally achieve a safe and efficient photocatalyst, which might be able to solve the environmental problem, energy shortage, and to realize the "end-of-pipe treatment" and "source control" at the same time.

Youzhou He: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Validation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Shuang Luo: Supervision, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Conceptualization. Yuwei Tan: Investigation, Formal analysis. Mingzuo Chen: Investigation, Formal analysis. Haibo Zhou: Investigation, Formal analysis. Jianning Yu: Investigation, Formal analysis. Linjiang Pu: Investigation, Formal analysis. Yumin Huang: Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Conceptualization. Min Fu: Formal analysis, Xingyan Liu: Writing – original draft, Project administration, Supervision.
